title: Who Is Behind Pixaroma? My Path from Design to YouTube
description:
Curious about the person behind Pixaroma? In this video, I share my journey from a passion for drawing to becoming a YouTube content creator. Discover how I transitioned from sketching to mastering design tools like Photoshop and Illustrator, and how AI has transformed my creative process. Learn about my background, the challenges I've overcome, and the tools I use to bring engaging content to life. Join me to uncover the story behind Pixaroma!

Join my Discord server: https://discord.gg/a8ZM7Qtsqq
Join my Facebook group: https://www.facebook.com/groups/pixaromacommunity

Chapters:
00:00 Introduction
00:13 Who Am I?
01:21 Educational Background
03:15 Professional Experience
03:42 My first Youtube
04:07 Pixaroma
04:20 Photography
04:51 Integrating AI & PC Specs
05:35 Pixaroma Channel Future Plans
transcript: Hello, in today's video, I want to talk about who is behind Pixaroma.  Have you ever wondered who I am, how I got into design, or how I started using AI,  and what led me to create a YouTube channel?  Stick around to find out all these answers and more.  Let's start with the beginning, but maybe let's not go too far back.  Obviously, I won that race.  My name is Dicee and I-O-N pronounced the Chiang Yuan.  I was born in 1981 and live in Romania.  Some of you might not have heard of Romania, but you probably know Dracula,  who is from Romania, or famous people like Nadia Cominich.  I learned English by watching movies with Romanian subtitles,  and later took some English courses.  I write well in English, but don't speak it as fluently,  since I don't have many opportunities to practice, except when I travel abroad.  That's why I use AI text-to-speech to convert my text into voice so everyone can understand.  Since I was a child, I've loved drawing.  In general school, I used to sketch and doodle on the last pages of my notebooks.  Back then, my drawings were simple lines without shadows and lights,  because there was no internet or YouTube to learn from.  In the army, I saw a tattoo magazine that inspired me to learn about shading and lighting.  This was the first time I seriously started improving my drawing skills.  After the army, I started working and also attended an IT university,  where I learned general computer knowledge and some programming.  I wanted to study design, but there wasn't a design university in my city until years later,  so I completed my IT degree, but realized design was my passion when we were asked to create simple  programs, and I made mine colorful and graphical.  Around 2007, I discovered Photoshop, probably CS2, and forums where I could learn more.  Despite my limited English, I experimented with tools and eventually became a moderator  on a Photoshop tutorial site. This was my first attempt at sharing what I had learned in creating  tutorials. I also started experimenting with digital painting and photo manipulations  using a mouse. In 2008, I started using Illustrator for logo design. Initially, I was not very good,  but I entered many logo design contests to practice and eventually won my first contest after  about 150 losses. I continued to improve and started winning more contests. I later created a  popular logo design group on Facebook with 50,000 members where I shared tips and engage with  the community. I took a graphic design course that lasted three years where I learned drawing,  Photoshop, Illustrator, Coral Draw, and 3D Studio Max. In my first year, I focused on drawing,  learning about perspective, composition, and observation. In the second year, I worked more with  digital tools using a small Wacom tablet and old CRT monitors. In the third year, I worked with  3D Studio Max and completed my diploma project, a fantasy card pack, which I later sold digitally.  After completing the course, I worked in mobile game design, creating interfaces, icons,  characters, and other game assets. Around 2014, 2015, I started selling illustrations on various  platforms like Shutterstock, Envato, Adobe Stock, Creative Market, and Design Bundles. I also  ventured into creating seamless patterns and SVG cut files for crafts, which became popular before  SVG files were widely used. I first attempted a YouTube channel named Pixoroma about eight years  ago, but abandoned it due to slow growth and the time-consuming process of making tutorials without  AI voices relying solely on text. You can still check it out if you want, but I changed the name to  Pixoroma Design, so I can use Pixoroma on this channel. Pixoroma combines the words  pixel and aroma, and I later created a company Pixoroma SRL, which is similar to an LTD.  I also started exploring Blender for 3D modeling and photography, taking a six-month course to  improve my skills. I use a Sony A6400 mirrorless camera with this lenses, Sony 1655mm F2.8G,  and 90mm F2.8G macro. Here are some self-portraits I took with my camera, so yeah, this is me having fun  with photography. When AI tools emerged, I experimented with a mid-journey stable diffusion,  Dal e, Adobe Firefly, and more, incorporating them into my workflow. However, AI impacted my  sales, which dropped significantly across all platforms. I share my earnings on my Facebook  group Pixoroma Community. If you're interested, I enjoy learning new technologies and finding ways  to integrate them into my work. To handle all my creative work, I use a high-performance PC. Here are  my current specs. The most important for AI work is the video card and the amount of VRAM.  In my case, I use an RTX 4090 with 24GB of VRAM. So the plan for the Pixoroma YouTube channel  is to share all the tools and processes I use in my design workflow. This includes AI for generating  images like stable diffusion and others that may appear in the future. AI for text like chat GPT  and possibly a voice generator like the one I use. Occasionally, I also create videos on  specific software I find interesting such as Photoshop or Illustrator and maybe some for animation  or video. Creating a video takes a lot of time as I cannot follow a script due to AI's  unpredictability. I record, edit, and adapt as I go using CapCut for editing and voice air AI  for voice. Creating videos like the ones I currently produce is very time consuming.  For an 8-minute tutorial, it takes me about 8 hours of work. Here's why it takes so much time.  Firstly, I can't script the process like others do. Now you press generate, now you get a cat,  etc. because AI is unpredictable. I generate something with AI and then proceed logically.  If it's a good generation, I might upscale or save it if not, I try in painting or a different  prompt. This adaptive process means I have to play around and try different approaches until it  works. Once I've recorded the video, I import it into CapCut desktop and start editing.  I watch the video in short segments, jotting down ideas about what to include.  After forming two, three sentences describing each segment, I write this in English,  though my grammar isn't perfect. I then ask chat GPT to correct it while keeping the meaning intact.  Next, I paste this text into the voice air platform, waiting a few seconds to generate the voice.  I listen to ensure there are no errors. If it's good, I download the voice. If not, I regenerate it.  Then, I sync the voice with the video in CapCut, adjusting playback speed or freezing frames as  needed to match the audio. I repeat this process throughout the video, adding transitions and  speeding up sections where I'm waiting for a generation or downloads. Once the video is ready,  I create a YouTube thumbnail, sometimes before the video itself. Finally, I title the video,  write a description with keywords and upload it to YouTube. It's a lengthy process and every  like, subscribe, or comment helps me a lot. My channel is monetized. Thanks to your support,  it's steadily growing. I hope it continues to grow in the future.  As of this recording, stable diffusion 3 has been released, showing good understanding  and vibrant colors, but struggling with people in hands. It's licensing isn't great,  and Sivit AI just banned the SD3 models. I'll explore it further and see how it develops.  Forge UI won't receive updates and will serve as beta testing, so I'll need to create more  tutorials on different interfaces soon. I enjoyed using Forge and also Automatic 1111, so I may  switch back to Automatic 1111 once it improves speed. For more complex tasks, I'm exploring  comfy UI. I still need more practice before tackling advanced tutorials, but we'll see how it goes.  Generally, I stick with AI that meets my needs efficiently. As you can see, I am a designer,  not a programmer, so I can help more with design than with programming errors and other technical  issues. Of course, if I know how to fix an error, I will tell you, but most of the time, I probably  won't have a solution. Thank you for taking the time to watch this video. I hope it helps the  community understand me better. Feel free to leave any questions in the comments on Facebook or  on Discord, thanks, and have a great day.
